name: Vilon
subtitle: KE Dipeptide | Thymus Immune Bioregulator
overview: Vilon is a Khavinson bioregulator dipeptide (KE) consisting of lysine and glutamic acid, originally isolated from thymus gland extracts. Developed by Professor Vladimir Khavinson at the St. Petersburg Institute of Bioregulation and Gerontology, it acts as a molecular signaling compound that restores normal gene expression and protein synthesis in immune and epithelial cells. Research shows Vilon increases mean lifespan by 20-40% in animal studies and suppresses tumor development.
mechanism: Vilon works through epigenetic modulation, restoring chromatin structure and gene expression in immune cells. It stimulates thymocyte proliferation, activates T-helper cells, and increases the proliferative index in thymus tissue (from 26% to 37% in studies). The peptide modulates tyrosine phosphorylation of mitogen-activated cytoplasmic kinases and helps rejuvenate aged immune systems by restoring proper gene expression patterns.
keyBenefits: Increases mean lifespan 20-40% in animal models
Restores chromatin structure in aged lymphocytes
Improves enzyme activity in aged tissue

items.name: Thymus Rejuvenation
items.description: Stimulates thymocyte proliferation and enlarges thymic lobules.

items.name: T-Cell Activation
items.description: Activates T-helper cells and improves immune response.

items.name: Age-Related Immune Decline
items.description: Restores chromatin structure and gene expression in aged lymphocytes.

items.name: Lifespan Extension
items.description: Long-term treatment increased mean lifespan 20-40% in rodent studies.

items.name: Tumor Suppression
items.description: Suppresses development of spontaneous and induced tumors.

items.name: Tissue Regeneration
items.description: Promotes recovery in tissues exposed to radiation damage.

items.name: Intestinal Function
items.description: Restores brush border membrane enzyme levels in aged animals.

items.name: Barrier Function
items.description: Enhances intestinal barrier function in aged rats.

deliveryMethods.overview: Available in capsule form for oral administration. As the shortest Khavinson peptide (just 2 amino acids), Vilon is easily absorbed orally. Typical protocol involves 10-20 day cycles, repeated 2-3 times per year.
Injectable form available for direct administration.

interactions.notes: Both target thymus/immune system; complementary mechanisms.
Often combined in comprehensive anti-aging Khavinson protocols.
Both have immunoprotecting properties; different mechanisms.
Related immune peptides; can be used in comprehensive protocols.

timeline.effects: Immune activation and gene expression modulation begins
Effects persist due to epigenetic changes
Cumulative benefits; longevity effects in animal models

references.title: Peptide Bioregulators Increase Lifespan
references.keyFindings: Long-term treatment with peptide preparations including Vilon increased mean lifespan 20-40% and suppressed tumorigenesis.

references.title: Vilon Effects on Aged Lymphocytes
references.journal: Bulletin of Experimental Biology and Medicine
references.keyFindings: Vilon improved lymphocytes from old people by restoring chromatin structure and gene expression.

references.title: Peptides Regulating Proliferative Activity in THP-1 Cells
references.keyFindings: Vilon and other Khavinson peptides modulate key proliferative patterns and tyrosine phosphorylation of kinases.

references.title: Short Peptide Immunoprotecting Properties
references.keyFindings: Vilon and related peptides possess age-associated immunoprotecting properties in the spleen.

quickStats.typicalDose: 10-20 mg per day (oral capsules) or 10 mg (injectable)
quickStats.frequency: Daily for 10-20 consecutive days (Khavinson bioregulator cycle)
quickStats.storage: Oral capsules: room temperature; Lyophilized powder/reconstituted: 2-8°C refrigerated
